quiz_questions.question_text: According to the kinetic theory of matter, all particles of matter are __________.

options.text: perfectly still at all times
constantly in motion at all times
motionless unless heated above 100 C
only moving in the gaseous state

quiz_questions.explanation: The kinetic theory of matter states that all particles of matter are in constant motion, regardless of the state of matter. Even solid particles vibrate in place.

quiz_questions.question_text: As temperature increases, what happens to the speed of particles?

options.text: The particles slow down as they absorb energy
The particles stop moving and become frozen
The particles speed up as they gain more energy
The speed of the particles remains the same

quiz_questions.explanation: When temperature increases, particles gain kinetic energy and move faster. Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of particles.

quiz_questions.question_text: In which state of matter do particles have the most kinetic energy?

options.text: Solid
Liquid
Gas
Crystal

quiz_questions.explanation: Gas particles move the fastest and have the most kinetic energy among the common states of matter. They move freely in all directions with high speeds.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do particles move in a solid?

options.text: They flow freely past one another in all directions
They vibrate in fixed positions but do not move around
They fly rapidly in straight lines between collisions
They do not move at all and have zero kinetic energy

quiz_questions.explanation: In a solid, particles are tightly packed and vibrate in fixed positions. They have kinetic energy but cannot move freely like particles in liquids or gases.

quiz_questions.question_text: What determines the state of matter a substance is in?

options.text: Only the color and size of the particles inside the substance
The kinetic energy and the forces between its particles
Only the shape of the container holding the substance inside
The chemical formula written for the substance on the label

quiz_questions.explanation: The state of matter depends on the balance between the kinetic energy of particles (how fast they move) and the attractive forces between them (how strongly they are held together).

quiz_questions.question_text: In which state do particles move freely and fill the entire container?

options.text: Solid only
Liquid only
Gas state
All states

quiz_questions.explanation: Gas particles have enough kinetic energy to overcome the attractive forces between them. They spread out and fill whatever container they are in.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to the space between particles as a substance changes from solid to liquid to gas?

options.text: The space between particles decreases with each change
The space between particles increases with each change
The space between particles always stays exactly the same
The space between particles first increases then decreases

quiz_questions.explanation: As substances change from solid to liquid to gas, particles gain energy and move farther apart. Gases have the most space between particles.

quiz_questions.question_text: According to kinetic theory, why do gases expand to fill their container?

options.text: Gas particles are attracted to the walls of the container strongly
Gas particles move fast and spread out in all available directions
Gases are pushed outward by the heavier air particles nearby
Gravity pulls gas particles to every surface of the container wall

quiz_questions.explanation: Gas particles have high kinetic energy and weak attractive forces between them, so they move rapidly in all directions and spread out to fill any available space.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement about liquid particles is correct?

options.text: They are locked in fixed positions and can only vibrate
They move freely in all directions like gas particles do
They slide past each other while staying relatively close
They have no kinetic energy and remain completely still

quiz_questions.explanation: Liquid particles have enough energy to slide past one another but not enough to completely break free from neighboring particles. This is why liquids flow but maintain a definite volume.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why does a solid have a definite shape according to kinetic theory?

options.text: Its particles move so fast they push against each other equally
Its particles have no energy and cannot move at all in any way
Its particles vibrate in fixed positions held by strong forces
Its particles are all the same size so they stack up neatly

quiz_questions.explanation: In a solid, strong attractive forces hold particles in fixed positions where they can only vibrate. This rigid arrangement gives solids their definite shape.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the kinetic theory of matter?

options.text: The theory that all particles of matter are in constant motion and their energy determines the state of matter
The theory that only gas particles are in motion while solid and liquid particles are completely still
The theory that matter can be created and destroyed during chemical reactions inside a laboratory
The theory that all matter is made up of elements that are listed in the periodic table of elements

quiz_questions.explanation: The kinetic theory of matter states that all matter is made of particles that are in constant motion. The speed of the particles and the forces between them determine the state of matter.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is kinetic energy in the context of particle motion?

options.text: The energy particles have because they are in motion
The energy stored in the bonds between atoms in a molecule
The energy needed to change a substance from one state to next
The energy released when a chemical reaction takes place now

quiz_questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is the energy of motion. Every moving particle has kinetic energy, and faster-moving particles have more kinetic energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'intermolecular forces' mean?

options.text: The forces of attraction between separate particles or molecules
The bonds that hold atoms together within a single molecule unit
The gravitational pull between the Earth and all matter on it
The pressure exerted by gas particles against container walls

quiz_questions.explanation: Intermolecular forces are the attractive forces that exist between separate molecules or particles. They influence whether a substance is a solid, liquid, or gas.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'random motion' mean for gas particles?

options.text: Gas particles move in organized patterns along predictable lines
Gas particles move in unpredictable directions at various speeds
Gas particles do not move at all inside of their container space
Gas particles move only when pushed by an outside force or wind

quiz_questions.explanation: Gas particles undergo random motion — they travel in unpredictable, straight-line paths in all directions at varying speeds, constantly bouncing off each other and container walls.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'elastic collision' mean in kinetic theory?

options.text: A collision where particles stick together and stop moving forward
A collision where no kinetic energy is lost and particles bounce apart
A collision where all kinetic energy is converted directly into heat
A collision where particles break apart into smaller separate pieces

quiz_questions.explanation: An elastic collision is one where the total kinetic energy is conserved — particles bounce off each other without losing energy. Ideal gas particles are assumed to have perfectly elastic collisions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is Brownian motion?

options.text: The random, zigzag movement of tiny particles caused by collisions with surrounding molecules
The organized circular flow of heated fluid moving upward and cooler fluid moving downward
The transfer of heat energy through electromagnetic waves traveling through empty space
The process by which a solid turns directly into a gas without first becoming a liquid state

quiz_questions.explanation: Brownian motion is the random, zigzag movement of small particles (like pollen in water) caused by constant bombardment from surrounding molecules. It provides evidence for the kinetic theory.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'diffusion' mean?

options.text: The freezing of a liquid into a solid structure
The spreading of particles from high to low concentration
The vibration of particles in a fixed solid position
The compression of gas into a smaller container space

quiz_questions.explanation: Diffusion is the process where particles spread out from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration due to their constant random motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'state of matter' mean?

options.text: The chemical formula that represents a particular pure substance
The physical form of matter determined by particle arrangement and motion
The temperature at which a substance begins to boil and evaporate away
The weight of a substance measured in grams on a laboratory balance

quiz_questions.explanation: A state of matter is a distinct physical form — solid, liquid, or gas — determined by how particles are arranged and how much kinetic energy they have.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'particle model' mean?

options.text: A scientific model that explains matter as being made of tiny moving particles
A physical replica of an atom built from clay or plastic for display purposes
A mathematical equation that calculates the mass of a chemical compound
A type of microscope used to view individual atoms on a smooth surface

quiz_questions.explanation: The particle model is a scientific model that represents all matter as being composed of tiny particles that are always moving. It helps explain the behavior of solids, liquids, and gases.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'phase change' mean?

options.text: When a substance transforms from one state of matter to another
When a substance changes its chemical identity during a reaction
When particles are created from energy during a nuclear process
When a gas is compressed until it becomes a completely new element

quiz_questions.explanation: A phase change is when a substance changes from one state of matter to another, such as melting (solid to liquid), boiling (liquid to gas), or freezing (liquid to solid).

quiz_questions.question_text: You open a bottle of perfume on one side of a room and soon people on the other side can smell it. What kinetic theory concept explains this?

options.text: Condensation, because the perfume vapor turns back into liquid droplets in air
Diffusion, because the perfume particles spread out through the air by moving randomly
Freezing, because the perfume particles slow down as they travel across the entire room
Filtration, because the air filters the perfume particles and carries them to the far side

quiz_questions.explanation: Diffusion explains this. The perfume molecules are in constant random motion and spread from the area of high concentration (the bottle) to lower concentration (across the room).

quiz_questions.question_text: A student notices that a drop of food coloring spreads faster in hot water than in cold water. How does kinetic theory explain this?

options.text: Hot water is less dense, which makes the food coloring float and spread on top
Cold water particles are frozen still, so they block the food coloring completely
Particles in hot water move faster, which speeds up the diffusion of the coloring
The food coloring reacts chemically with hot water but not with the cold water

quiz_questions.explanation: Higher temperature means particles have more kinetic energy and move faster. The faster-moving water particles collide with the food coloring more frequently, spreading it more quickly.

quiz_questions.question_text: A balloon is inflated and left in a warm room. When the balloon is moved to a cold room, it appears to shrink. What explains this?

options.text: Gas leaked out of the balloon through tiny holes in its rubber surface
The cold room compressed the rubber and made it contract around the gas
The gas particles slowed down in the cold and exerted less outward pressure
Cold air from outside entered the balloon and pushed the warm air particles out

quiz_questions.explanation: In the cold room, gas particles lose kinetic energy and slow down. They hit the balloon walls less forcefully and less often, reducing pressure and causing the balloon to shrink.

quiz_questions.question_text: A scientist observes tiny pollen grains jerking around randomly when viewed under a microscope in water. What is this evidence of?

options.text: The pollen grains are alive and swimming through the water on their own
Water molecules are constantly moving and bumping into the pollen grains
Static electricity in the water is pushing the pollen in random directions
The microscope's light source is vibrating and causing the pollen to shake

quiz_questions.explanation: This is Brownian motion. The invisible water molecules are in constant motion and collide with the visible pollen grains, pushing them in random zigzag patterns.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why can you walk through air easily but not through a brick wall, according to kinetic theory?

options.text: Air particles have spaces between them, but brick particles are tightly packed together with strong forces
Air is not actually made of particles so there is nothing for your body to push against at all
Brick walls contain heavier particles that push back against you when you try to walk forward
Air particles move out of the way because they are scared of larger objects moving nearby

quiz_questions.explanation: Gas particles in air are far apart with weak intermolecular forces, so they easily move aside. Solid brick particles are tightly packed with strong forces holding them in rigid positions.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student heats a solid until it melts. Using kinetic theory, what happened to the particles?

options.text: The particles shrank in size and slipped through each other freely
The particles gained enough energy to break free from fixed positions and flow
The particles lost energy and moved closer together forming a liquid mass
The particles were destroyed by the heat and reformed as a different material

quiz_questions.explanation: When a solid melts, the added heat energy increases the kinetic energy of particles enough to partially overcome the intermolecular forces, allowing them to break free and slide past each other.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why does a gas fill its entire container but a liquid pools at the bottom?

options.text: Gas particles have enough energy to overcome all attractive forces and spread out completely
Liquid particles are heavier than gas particles, which is why they always sink to the bottom
Gas particles are pushed to the top by gravity while liquid particles are held down tightly
Liquids have no kinetic energy at all, so their particles cannot rise above the bottom edge

quiz_questions.explanation: Gas particles have enough kinetic energy to completely overcome intermolecular forces and fill the container. Liquid particles still have some attraction to each other, so they stay close and are pulled down by gravity.

quiz_questions.question_text: A teacher demonstrates that a syringe filled with air can be compressed, but a syringe filled with water cannot. Why?

options.text: Water is heavier than air so it takes more force to push the plunger down fully
Air particles are far apart and can be pushed closer; water particles are already close together
The syringe has a leak that lets air escape but keeps the water trapped inside tightly
Water creates a vacuum seal inside the syringe that prevents the plunger from moving

quiz_questions.explanation: According to kinetic theory, gas particles have large spaces between them and can be pushed closer together (compressed). Liquid particles are already close together with little space to compress.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student notices that sugar dissolves faster when stirred into hot tea than cold tea. How does kinetic theory explain both factors?

options.text: Stirring and heat both increase particle motion, helping the sugar dissolve more quickly
Stirring breaks the sugar into smaller chemical compounds, and heat melts those compounds
Hot tea has fewer particles than cold tea, creating more room for sugar to fit inside it
Sugar only dissolves in hot liquids; stirring just mixes it in but it does not truly dissolve

quiz_questions.explanation: Both stirring and higher temperature increase the kinetic energy and movement of particles. Faster-moving water particles collide with sugar more often, breaking it apart and dissolving it faster.

quiz_questions.question_text: A frozen pond has ice on top but liquid water below. Using kinetic theory, why do the particles at the surface form a solid while those below remain liquid?

options.text: The surface particles are in contact with cold air and lost enough energy to lock into fixed positions
The bottom particles are being heated by the sun shining through the ice above them at all times
Water at the bottom is a different chemical substance than the ice floating on the surface above
Gravity pulls the faster particles to the bottom and pushes the slower particles to the top edge

quiz_questions.explanation: The surface water is exposed to the cold air and loses thermal energy. When particles lose enough kinetic energy, intermolecular forces lock them into fixed positions, forming ice.
